question: [File: 'File Name: rest/service/cryptoAllowanceService.js -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: getAccountCryptoAllowances()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the `select *` query in accountAllowanceQuery to retrieve all columns including payer_account_id, owner, spender, amount, amount_granted, and timestamp_range simultaneously, enabling the attacker to reconstruct the complete authorization chain (who approved, who pays, who spends, how much, when) for any account without any owner consent or authentication?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/cryptoAllowanceService.js -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: getAccountAllowancesQuery()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that getAccountAllowancesQuery() accepts the whereConditions array by reference and does not validate that the ownerCondition (owner = $1) is always present in the array, so that if the controller omits the owner condition due to a bug, the service executes a full table scan returning all allowances across all accounts without any owner-based authorization check?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/cryptoAllowanceService.js -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: getAccountCryptoAllowances()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the absence of authentication in getAccountCryptoAllowances() to perform a timing-based oracle attack by querying allowances for many accounts and observing response times, inferring which accounts have large numbers of allowances (indicating high-value targets) without any owner approval of this reconnaissance?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/cryptoAllowanceService.js -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: getAccountAllowancesQuery()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the `params` array is mutated by reference in getAccountAllowancesQuery() (`const params = whereParams; params.push(limit)`), causing the limit value to be appended to the shared params array, which if the same array is reused across multiple service calls in a concurrent environment, could shift parameter positions and cause the owner filter to bind to the wrong value, returning another account's allowances?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/cryptoAllowanceService.js -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: getAccountCryptoAllowances()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that getAccountCryptoAllowances() exposes the spender field for all allowances of a target account, enabling the attacker to identify every account that has been granted spending authority, then impersonate or target those spenders to execute unauthorized transfers using the discovered allowances without the owner's approval of the disclosure?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/cryptoAllowanceService.js -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: getAccountAllowancesQuery()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the `whereConditions.length > 0` check in getAccountAllowancesQuery() by crafting a scenario where the conditions array contains only whitespace or empty string conditions that pass the length check but produce an invalid WHERE clause, causing the database to ignore the owner filter and return all allowances without owner-based authorization?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/cryptoAllowanceService.js -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: getAccountCryptoAllowances()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the lack of caller identity binding in getAccountCryptoAllowances() to query allowances for a victim account immediately after a known allowance approval transaction is confirmed on-chain, enabling real-time monitoring of newly granted spending authority without the owner's consent, and using that information to front-run the authorized spender?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/cryptoAllowanceService.js -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: getAccountAllowancesQuery()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that getAccountAllowancesQuery() uses `select * from crypto_allowance` without column restriction, so that any future addition of sensitive columns (e.g., private keys, internal flags, authorization tokens) to the crypto_allowance table would be automatically exposed to any unauthenticated caller, creating a persistent authorization bypass risk without owner approval?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/cryptoAllowanceService.js -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: getAccountCryptoAllowances()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the absence of any request authentication in getAccountCryptoAllowances() to systematically query allowances for all accounts in a known account range, building a complete network-wide map of delegated spending authority that reveals which accounts have authorized others to transact on their behalf, without any owner approval of this mass disclosure?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/cryptoAllowanceService.js -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: getAccountAllowancesQuery()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the limit parameter is pushed directly into the params array in getAccountAllowancesQuery() without validation at the service layer, so that a negative or zero limit value could cause unexpected database behavior (e.g., returning all rows or erroring), bypassing the intended pagination restriction and enabling bulk extraction of allowance authorization data?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/cryptoAllowanceService.js -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: getAccountCryptoAllowances()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the `rows.map((ca) => new CryptoAllowance(ca))` mapping in getAccountCryptoAllowances(), which uses `mapKeys` with camelCase conversion on all database columns, to receive a fully hydrated object containing all raw database fields including internal accounting fields (amount, amount_granted, payer_account_id), enabling the attacker to determine the exact financial authorization state without owner consent?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/cryptoAllowanceService.js -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: getAccountAllowancesQuery()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that getAccountAllowancesQuery() does not enforce a minimum set of required conditions (specifically the ownerCondition), so that a caller that passes an empty conditions array receives a query with no WHERE clause, executing `SELECT * FROM crypto_allowance ORDER BY spender LIMIT $1` and returning all allowances across all accounts, authorizing the attacker to see every spending delegation on the network?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/cryptoAllowanceService.js -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: getAccountCryptoAllowances()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that getAccountCryptoAllowances() does not verify that the accountId in the request path corresponds to the authenticated caller, enabling any user to query allowances for any other account (e.g., GET /api/v1/accounts/0.0.1234/allowances/crypto), disclosing who has been authorized to spend on behalf of account 0.0.1234 without that account owner's approval of the disclosure?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/cryptoAllowanceService.js -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: getAccountAllowancesQuery()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the order parameter passed to OrderSpec.from(CryptoAllowance.SPENDER, order) in getAccountAllowancesQuery() by injecting a crafted order string that modifies the ORDER BY clause to include additional columns or expressions, potentially causing information leakage about the internal database schema or enabling side-channel inference of allowance data beyond what the owner authorized for disclosure?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/cryptoAllowanceService.js -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: getAccountCryptoAllowances()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that getAccountCryptoAllowances() returns allowance records sorted by spender ID, enabling the attacker to use binary search with spender.id range filters (gt/lt) to efficiently enumerate all spenders for a target account in O(log n) queries rather than O(n), dramatically accelerating unauthorized reconnaissance of the owner's authorization decisions?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/cryptoAllowanceService.js -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: getAccountAllowancesQuery()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that getAccountAllowancesQuery() builds the final SQL by joining an array of string conditions with ' and ', where each condition is a raw string fragment, so that if any condition string contains a closing parenthesis or SQL keyword injected through a malformed filter, the resulting query could escape the WHERE clause context and execute arbitrary SQL, bypassing the owner authorization check entirely?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/cryptoAllowanceService.js -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: getAccountCryptoAllowances()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the lack of authentication in getAccountCryptoAllowances() to monitor allowance changes over time by polling the endpoint repeatedly, detecting when new spenders are added or existing allowances are modified, enabling real-time surveillance of an account owner's authorization decisions without their knowledge or consent?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/cryptoAllowanceService.js -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: getAccountAllowancesQuery()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the `select * from ${CryptoAllowance.tableName}` template literal in accountAllowanceQuery, where CryptoAllowance.tableName is a static class property set to 'crypto_allowance', to verify that the query always targets the live allowance table rather than a historical snapshot, enabling the attacker to always see the most current authorization state including recently approved allowances before the owner can revoke them?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/cryptoAllowanceService.js -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: getAccountCryptoAllowances()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the absence of any IP-based or account-based rate limiting in getAccountCryptoAllowances() to send thousands of concurrent requests for different account IDs, causing a denial-of-service condition on the database while simultaneously extracting allowance authorization data for all queried accounts without any owner approval?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/cryptoAllowanceService.js -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: getAccountAllowancesQuery()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that getAccountAllowancesQuery() does not validate the type or content of the whereConditions array elements before joining them into the SQL query, so that a non-string element (e.g., an object with a toString() method that returns a SQL injection payload) could produce a malformed WHERE clause that bypasses the owner filter and returns unauthorized allowance data?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/cryptoAllowanceService.js -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: getAccountCryptoAllowances()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that getAccountCryptoAllowances() exposes the payer_account_id for each allowance, which may be a different account from the owner (e.g., a fee-paying delegate), enabling the attacker to identify accounts that are paying transaction fees on behalf of others and target those fee-payer accounts for exploitation, without the owner's approval of this relationship disclosure?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/cryptoAllowanceService.js -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: getAccountAllowancesQuery()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the amountCondition (`amount > 0`) is the only business logic filter in getAccountAllowancesQuery() and is appended unconditionally at the end of the conditions array, meaning that if the conditions array already contains a conflicting `amount` condition (e.g., `amount > -1`), the AND combination may produce unexpected results depending on database query optimization, potentially exposing zero-amount (revoked) allowances as if they were still active authorizations?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/cryptoAllowanceService.js -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: getAccountCryptoAllowances()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that getAccountCryptoAllowances() does not check whether the queried account exists before executing the allowance query, enabling the attacker to probe for account existence by observing whether the response returns an empty allowances array versus a 404 error, leaking account existence information without owner consent?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/cryptoAllowanceService.js -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: getAccountAllowancesQuery()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that getAccountAllowancesQuery() uses a single shared `accountAllowanceQuery` static string (`select * from crypto_allowance`) that is never modified, meaning there is no per-request query isolation, and if the database connection pool reuses prepared statement caches, a crafted request could potentially influence the query plan used for another user's allowance query, causing cross-request data leakage?
